calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Granting Relief From Default And Default Judgment
Off Calendar Nov-04-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Lisa Meier of the law offices of LISA B. MEIER (lisa@jacksonsquarelaw.com) specially appears as counsel for plaintiff

No other appearance is made in this matter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT G&Z INC.'s Motion For Order Granting Relief From Default And Default Judgment, to set aside the default entered against it on February 28, 2020 and the judgment entered on March 13, 2020 is denied as untimely. A motion to set aside a default brought under Code of Civil Procedure Section 473(b) must be filed within six months. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 473(b).) "The six-month limit is mandatory; a court has no authority to grant relief under section 473, subdivision (b), unless an application is made within the six-month period." (Arumbula v. Union Carbide Corp. (2005) 128 Cal.App.4th 333, 340.) The Court has no authority to grant relief from the six-month limit, whether based on mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect or on an attorney's affidavit of fault. (Id. at 345; accord, Austin v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 918, 918 ["The six-month time limitation is jurisdictional; the court has no power to grant relief under section 473 once the time has lapsed."].) Defendant did not file its motion until September 18, 2020, more than six months after both the default and the default judgment were entered. Defendant's unsupported speculation that the default judgment was entered "on some date after May 27, 2020" and "back-dated" to March 13, 2020 is just that. Even if the judgment did not appear on the Court's Register of Actions for some time due to a delay in processing e-filings caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, Defendant admits its counsel was aware by March 2020 that its default had been taken on February 28, yet it did not attempt to move to set aside the default until September 11. It offers no explanation for the lengthy delay. "Where a party has filed a motion for relief after an extended delay without any adequate excuse therefor, it is an abuse of discretion for a trial court to grant relief under section 473." (Stafford v. Mach (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1174, 1183 [delays of three months or more routinely result in denial of relief where there is no satisfactory explanation for the delay].) The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
